audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed as Bildad's first speech: accusation of wind-words, traditional retribution theology, appeal to ancestors, papyrus/reed metaphor, forgotten-God claim, and restoration promise. Marked as Bildad's speech, not final theology.

verses.restored: Bildad the Shuhite answered and said:
How long will you speak these things, and the words of your mouth be a mighty wind?
Does the Divine twist justice? Does Shaddai twist righteousness?
If your children sinned against him, he sent them into the hand of their transgression.
If you would seek the Divine and plead for favor from Shaddai,
if you are pure and upright, surely now he would awaken for you and restore your righteous dwelling.
Though your beginning was small, your latter end would grow very great.
For ask, please, the former generation, and establish yourself by the search of their fathers.
For we are yesterday and know nothing, because our days on earth are a shadow.
Will they not teach you, speak to you, and bring words out from their heart?
Can papyrus grow where there is no marsh? Can reeds grow without water?
While still in its greenness, not cut down, it withers before any other grass.
So are the paths of all who forget the Divine; the hope of the godless shall perish.
His confidence is cut off, and his trust is a spider’s house.
He leans on his house, but it does not stand; he holds fast to it, but it does not endure.
He is fresh before the sun, and his shoot goes out over his garden.
His roots wrap around the stone heap; he sees the house of stones.
If he is swallowed from his place, it will deny him, saying, I have not seen you.
Look, this is the joy of his way, and from the dust others will sprout.
Look, the Divine will not reject a whole person, nor will he grasp the hand of evildoers.
He will yet fill your mouth with laughter and your lips with shouting.
Those who hate you will be clothed with shame, and the tent of the wicked will be no more.
